title: Learning Mission-Oriented Ecosystem Engineering
text:
Course Overview
This course teaches learners to define, model, simulate, and evaluate a Mission-Oriented Ecosystem Engineering (MOEE) system. The running case is a campus food-service ecosystem, but the method transfers to elderly services, education, health care, public transportation, and home-based business.
The course keeps three distinctions visible from the beginning:

title: Learning Mission-Oriented Ecosystem Engineering
text:
Audience and prerequisites
The course is intended for advanced undergraduate students, graduate students, public-service designers, systems engineers, policy analysts, and software practitioners. Learners should be able to read basic algebra and simple Python. Prior knowledge of artificial intelligence, economics, or simulation is helpful but not required.

title: Learning Mission-Oriented Ecosystem Engineering
text:
Assessment

Concept checks and short exercises: 15%
Modeling worksheet: 20%
Python labs: 30%
Capstone case, simulation, and defense: 35%

The capstone is assessed on conceptual consistency, traceability from public need to code, experimental validity, reproducibility, and appropriate treatment of safety and stakeholder autonomy.

title: Learning Mission-Oriented Ecosystem Engineering
text:
Repository resources

Manuscript: ../paper.qmd
Python package: ../simulation/
Provider configuration: ../simulation/.env.example
Executable labs: labs/
Blank modeling templates: worksheets.qmd

Render this course from the repository root with:
quarto render course

title: 1  MOEE Foundations
text:
1.1 Why ecosystem engineering?
A public need is rarely delivered by one organization. Food access, health care, mobility, education, elderly care, and local enterprise depend on stakeholders with different resources, time horizons, authority, and definitions of value. MOEE treats this network as an engineered ecosystem without pretending that it is a single firm or that stakeholders surrender their autonomy.
The modeling objective is not merely to maximize transactions. It is to make the following chain explicit and testable:
[ . ]

title: 1  MOEE Foundations
text:
1.2 Core model








Element
Question answered
Typical artifact




Stakeholder classes
Who uses, sources, governs, and operates the ecosystem?
Stakeholder registry


Missions
What must each stakeholder accomplish?
Mission statements and constraints


MIG
When and how intensely is mission capacity used?
(I_i(t))


PSKVE
What multidimensional exchanges occur?
Product–Service–Knowledge–Value–Environment matrix


VOMR
What state and wealth changes are recorded?
Value–Output–Mission–Resource accounts


PUDAL
How are state, policy, learning, and control connected?
Feedback-control map


Triune Intelligence
Which forms of intelligence inform control?
HNI–CCI–AI evidence map


Triple Markets
How are assets, liquidity, and operations separated?
Stock–Financial–R–O mapping

title: 1  MOEE Foundations
text:
1.3 Stakeholder ontology
Every case begins with four functional roles:

User stakeholders consume or benefit from mission outputs.
Source stakeholders supply resources, products, services, or knowledge.
Regulator/governance stakeholders define and enforce rules, rights, and public objectives.
Operator stakeholders schedule, match, route, settle, or maintain ecosystem activity.

An organization may occupy several roles. The model should represent each role explicitly when its mission, constraints, or accounts differ.

title: 1  MOEE Foundations
text:
1.4 Mission Intensity Graphs
For stakeholder (i), the MIG is
[ I_i(t), ]
where zero means inactive mission capacity and one means fully scheduled capacity. A simple output relationship is
[ O_i(t)=f_i(R_i(t),C_i,E_i(t))I_i(t). ]
The MIG is not merely a demand curve. Depending on the stakeholder, it may represent care visits, teaching load, regulatory inspection, vehicle deployment, production, platform throughput, or another mission-specific capacity.

title: 1  MOEE Foundations
text:
1.5 Two independent design axes

1.5.1 Reasoning axis ()

Historical: replay or deterministic rules.
Probabilistic: sample from uncertainty-aware models.
Agentic: reason from goals, beliefs, observations, and constraints.
Hybrid: combine several reasoners with explicit weights or gates.



1.5.2 Setup axis ()

Independent: stakeholders commit their own MIGs.
Smart contract: stakeholders prenegotiate a fixed coordination rule.
Adaptive cooperative: PUDAL recommends revisions using Triune Intelligence and current feedback.

Keeping these axes separate prevents a common error: attributing an improvement to TISE when it actually came from giving one model a more capable stakeholder reasoner.

title: 1  MOEE Foundations
text:
1.6 PSKVE and VOMR
PSKVE describes what crosses stakeholder boundaries:

Products
Services
Knowledge
Values
Environment

VOMR describes what is accounted for:

Value
Output
Mission
Resources

The causal transformation is (RMOV), while VOMR is the reporting order. Financial margin is only one component of systemic value:
[ V_i=_i-_i. ]
Mission achievement, externalities, equity, safety, and resilience must remain visible rather than being silently collapsed into profit.

title: 1  MOEE Foundations
text:
1.7 Triune Intelligence and PUDAL
Triune Intelligence combines:

Human/Natural Intelligence (HNI)
Crowd/Cultural/Collective Intelligence (CCI)
Artificial Intelligence (AI)

PUDAL uses these channels to perceive state, interpret missions and policy, recommend coordinated actions, and learn from outcomes. AI is one input—not the sovereign objective setter. High-stakes decisions still require domain authority, consent, auditability, and appropriate human judgment.

title: 1  MOEE Foundations
text:
1.8 Three validation claims

Baseline: validates that stakeholders, missions, MIGs, VOMR accounts, and wealth creation form a coherent executable ecosystem.
SOTA: validates that a PSKVE Exchange Engine and prenegotiated smart contract can improve a chosen objective relative to baseline.
TISE: tests whether adaptive cooperative PUDAL control improves value creation and withstands disruptions better than the strongest fixed alternative.

title: 1  MOEE Foundations
text:
1.9 Concept check
For a public-transport case, identify one stakeholder for each role, give each one a possible MIG, and explain why passenger demand and fleet deployment should not be represented by the same MIG.

title: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case
text:
2.1 Step 1 — State the public need and boundary
This tutorial uses campus food service as the worked case. Replace the worked entries with your own case while preserving the sequence and traceability.
Write one sentence describing the need, population, place, and time horizon.
Boundary decisions:
Acceptance check: every modeled exchange and metric must be relevant to this boundary.
crumbs: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case

title: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case
text:
The campus community needs safe, nutritious, affordable meals during operating days while vendors remain viable and waste and disruption losses remain acceptable.



Included: students, vendors, suppliers, campus regulators, service operators, ordering and delivery.
Excluded in the first model: regional agricultural policy and long-term campus construction.
Time step: one service period.
Horizon: 24 service periods.
crumbs: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case

title: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case
text:
2.2 Step 2 — Register stakeholder roles









ID
Functional role
Worked stakeholder
Why it is needed




users
User
Students
Consume meals and express demand and affordability needs


sources
Source
Food vendors and suppliers
Transform ingredients and labor into meals


regulators
Regulator/governance
Campus food authority
Enforces safety, access, and sustainability policy


operators
Operator
Campus food-service operator
Coordinates schedules, ordering, settlement, and delivery



Do not add a stakeholder merely because it exists in reality. Add it when it owns a distinct mission, constraint, decision, exchange, or VOMR account.
crumbs: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case

title: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case
text:
2.3 Step 3 — Define missions and constraints
Use this pattern:

Verb + mission output + beneficiary + timing + constraints.

Worked missions:

Users: obtain affordable, nutritious meals during required service periods.
Sources: produce safe meals while maintaining positive operating value.
Regulators: maintain safety and sustainability requirements.
Operators: provide reliable matching, scheduling, and settlement.

For each mission, record hard constraints separately from preferences. Safety limits are not weights that may be traded away for profit.
crumbs: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case

title: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case
text:
2.4 Step 4 — Open VOMR accounts










Stakeholder
Value (V)
Output (O)
Mission (M)
Resources (R)




Users
affordability/utility account
meals accessed
access achievement
budget, time, information


Sources
operating value
meals produced
production achievement
ingredients, labor, assets


Regulators
public-value account
inspections/policy services
compliance achievement
staff, authority, knowledge


Operators
service value
orders routed/settled
reliability achievement
platform, staff, liquidity



Specify units and update rules. If an account cannot be updated from simulation events, it is still a concept—not yet a computational variable.
crumbs: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case

title: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case
text:
2.5 Step 5 — Map PSKVE exchanges
Create one row per directed exchange.











Source → destination
Product
Service
Knowledge
Value
Environment




Suppliers → vendors
Ingredients
Replenishment
Availability data
Payment claim
Cold-chain conditions


Vendors → users
Meals
Preparation
Nutrition/allergen data
Affordability and revenue
Packaging and waste


Users → operators
—
Order request
Demand and feedback
Payment
Queue conditions


Regulators → ecosystem
—
Inspection
Rules and alerts
Safety assurance
Facility constraints



Check that every critical resource and output has a corresponding exchange path.
crumbs: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case

title: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case
text:
2.6 Step 6 — Define stakeholder MIGs
For each stakeholder, complete:



Field
Example




MIG meaning
Fraction of vendor meal-production capacity scheduled


Time step
One meal-service period


Zero
No production scheduled


One
Full feasible production capacity


Inputs
demand, stock, staffing, disruption, policy


Hard limits
ingredients, labor, safety, liquidity


Reasoner
historical, probabilistic, agentic, or hybrid



Use different symbols or stakeholder IDs even when MIGs interact. A user demand MIG and vendor production MIG may correlate, but they are not interchangeable.
crumbs: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case

title: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case
text:
2.7 Step 7 — Select setup modes
Model all three for a fair comparison:

Baseline: each stakeholder commits its proposal independently.
SOTA: stakeholders prenegotiate a smart contract, objective, and fixed coordination rule.
TISE: stakeholders cooperate through adaptive PUDAL recommendations informed by Triune Intelligence.

Write the prenegotiated contract before running SOTA. Changing it in response to a simulated shock would quietly turn it into an adaptive model.
crumbs: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case

title: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case
crumbs: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case

title: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case
crumbs: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case

title: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case
text:
2.10 Step 10 — Write validation questions

VQ1: Does the baseline execute valid stakeholder MIGs and balanced VOMR updates?
VQ2: Does each fixed SOTA contract improve its declared objective over baseline?
VQ3: Does TISE match or exceed the strongest SOTA value result under normal operation?
VQ4: Does TISE retain more performance or recover faster under disruption?
crumbs: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case

Public need

Affordable meals
User access

user O/M/V
affordability, access
Stakeholder, Mission


Reliable supply
Vendor viability

source R/O/V

Safe operation

If a requirement has no code object or metric, the implementation is incomplete. If code has no requirement, it may be accidental complexity.
crumbs: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case

title: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case
text:
2.12 Step 12 — Approve the model specification
Before coding, review the model with representatives of all stakeholder classes. Record unresolved disagreements about missions, weights, data, authority, and acceptable automation. In public-need ecosystems, disagreement is model input—not noise to delete.
crumbs: 2  Tutorial: Build an MOEE Model for a Specific Case

title: 3  Step-by-Step Python Simulation Development
text:
3.1 Step 0 — Verify Python and tests
The repository already contains a standard-library implementation in ./simulation/. This chapter reconstructs the development sequence so learners understand and can replace each layer.
simulation
Run all commands from the repository root.
No external Python package is required for the core simulator.

title: 3  Step-by-Step Python Simulation Development
text:
python --version
python -m unittest discover -s simulation/tests -v

title: 3  Step-by-Step Python Simulation Development
section: 3.2 Step 1 — Create a mission and VOMR account
text:
3.2 Step 1 — Create a mission and VOMR account
from simulation.models import Mission, VOMRAccount

mission = Mission(
    name="Reliable meal production",
    target_output_per_step=55,
    resource_per_output=1.0,
    value_per_output=1.2,
    resource_unit_cost=0.3,
)

accounts = VOMRAccount(value=0, output=0, mission=0, resources=120)
Mission holds stable transformation parameters. VOMRAccount holds changing state. Keeping them separate makes calibration and experiment reset easier.

title: 3  Step-by-Step Python Simulation Development
text:
3.3 Step 2 — Choose a stakeholder reasoner
Historical reasoning:
from simulation.reasoning import HistoricalReasoner

reasoner = HistoricalReasoner([0.35, 0.50, 0.80, 0.65])
Probabilistic reasoning:
from simulation.reasoning import ProbabilisticReasoner

reasoner = ProbabilisticReasoner(mean=0.58, stddev=0.08, seed=42)
Agentic reasoning:
from simulation.providers import ProviderFactory
from simulation.reasoning import AgentReasoner

provider = ProviderFactory.create("heuristic")
reasoner = AgentReasoner(provider)
The heuristic provider is offline and auditable. Replace "heuristic" with "ollama", "openai", or "deepseek" after configuring credentials.

title: 3  Step-by-Step Python Simulation Development
text:
3.4 Step 3 — Construct a stakeholder
from simulation.models import Stakeholder, StakeholderRole

vendor = Stakeholder(
    stakeholder_id="sources",
    name="Food vendors",
    role=StakeholderRole.SOURCE,
    mission=mission,
    capacity=90,
    accounts=accounts,
    reasoner=reasoner,
    resource_replenishment=45,
)
Create equivalent objects for user, regulator, and operator roles. Each needs its own mission and VOMR account even if several roles belong to one institution.

title: 3  Step-by-Step Python Simulation Development
text:
3.5 Step 4 — Define a scenario
from simulation.models import Scenario

scenario = Scenario(
    name="shortage",
    demand_profile=(0.35, 0.55, 0.85, 0.75),
    disruption_profile=(0.0, 0.0, 0.7, 0.3),
    description="A source shock during peak demand",
)
Profiles repeat if the run is longer than the tuple. Use normalized values in ([0,1]).

title: 3  Step-by-Step Python Simulation Development
text:
3.6 Step 5 — Select the ecosystem setup
Baseline:
from simulation.coordination import IndependentCoordinator
coordinator = IndependentCoordinator()
SOTA:
from simulation.coordination import SmartContractCoordinator, SmartContractTerms

coordinator = SmartContractCoordinator(
    SmartContractTerms(
        intensity_caps={"sources": 0.90},
        role_multipliers={"source": 1.05, "operator": 1.08},
        max_total_intensity=3.2,
    )
)
TISE:
from simulation.coordination import AdaptivePUDALCoordinator
coordinator = AdaptivePUDALCoordinator(provider, allow_fallback=True)
The smart contract is immutable during a run. PUDAL may adapt, but its recommendations remain bounded by resource feasibility and stakeholder policy.

title: 3  Step-by-Step Python Simulation Development
text:
3.7 Step 6 — Add the Triple-Market system
from simulation.markets import TripleMarketSystem

markets = TripleMarketSystem()
signals = markets.discover(demand=0.8, disruption=0.6)
print(signals.stock.availability)
print(signals.financial.availability)
print(signals.resource_output.price_multiplier)
The reference markets are deliberately simple. A case-specific model may subclass Market and replace its discovery equation.

title: 3  Step-by-Step Python Simulation Development
section: 3.8 Step 7 — Assemble and run the simulation
text:
3.8 Step 7 — Assemble and run the simulation
from simulation.engine import MOEESimulation, SimulationConfig

simulation = MOEESimulation(
    stakeholders=[user, vendor, regulator, operator],
    scenario=scenario,
    coordinator=coordinator,
    config=SimulationConfig(steps=24, seed=42),
)

result = simulation.run()
print(result.summary())
result.to_csv("results/my_case.csv")
The complete runnable demo factory avoids repeating boilerplate:
python -m simulation --architecture tise --reasoning hybrid --provider heuristic --steps 24 --output results/tise.csv

title: 3  Step-by-Step Python Simulation Development
section: 3.9 Step 8 — Configure an external agent provider
text:
3.9 Step 8 — Configure an external agent provider
Copy simulation/.env.example to .env and configure only one provider.
Ollama:
python -m simulation --architecture tise --reasoning agent --provider ollama --steps 6
OpenAI:
$env:OPENAI_API_KEY="your-key"
python -m simulation --architecture tise --reasoning agent --provider openai --steps 6
DeepSeek:
$env:DEEPSEEK_API_KEY="your-key"
python -m simulation --architecture tise --reasoning agent --provider deepseek --steps 6
External agent runs may make several requests per step: one per agent-reasoned stakeholder plus an adaptive PUDAL request. Begin with a short horizon, inspect decisions, and never commit credentials.

title: 3  Step-by-Step Python Simulation Development
3  Step-by-Step Python Simulation Development
section: 3.11 Step 10 — Extend to a new case

title: 3  Step-by-Step Python Simulation Development
text:
3.12 Executable labs

labs/lab01_baseline.py
labs/lab02_compare_architectures.py
labs/lab03_agentic_tise.py
labs/lab04_custom_case.py

title: 4  Experiments, Evidence, and Interpretation
text:
4.1 Factorial design
At minimum, cross these factors:
Do not compare historical baseline with agentic TISE and attribute the entire difference to PUDAL. Hold the reasoning mode constant when estimating the effect of setup.

title: 4  Experiments, Evidence, and Interpretation
text:
Factor
Levels




Architecture/setup
Baseline, SOTA, TISE


MIG reasoning
Historical, probabilistic, agentic, hybrid


Scenario
Normal and named disruptions


Objective
Value, mission, equity, sustainability, resilience


Seed
Multiple seeds for stochastic modes

title: 4  Experiments, Evidence, and Interpretation
text:
4.2 Minimum experiment sequence

Run baseline/historical to validate entities, flows, and VOMR conservation.
Run baseline/probabilistic across seeds to validate uncertainty handling.
Run SOTA with each prenegotiated objective.
Run TISE under the same normal scenarios.
Repeat all architectures under disruptions.
Compare TISE with the strongest SOTA configuration, not merely the weakest.

title: 4  Experiments, Evidence, and Interpretation
text:
4.3 Metrics

4.3.1 Value creation
[ V_i=_i-_i ]
Report stakeholder-level and ecosystem aggregates. A positive total can coexist with an unacceptable loss for one stakeholder.


4.3.2 Mission achievement
[ A_i(t)=(1,). ]


4.3.3 Performance retention
[ Retention=. ]


4.3.4 Recovery time
Count time steps from disruption onset until performance returns to a declared fraction of its normal reference.


4.3.5 Cumulative disruption loss
[ L=t(0,Performance{normal}(t)-Performance_{disrupted}(t)). ]

title: 4  Experiments, Evidence, and Interpretation
text:
4.4 Reproducible batch exercise
Run each command with the same step count and seed:
python -m simulation --architecture baseline --reasoning probabilistic --steps 24 --seed 42 --output results/baseline.csv
python -m simulation --architecture sota --reasoning probabilistic --steps 24 --seed 42 --output results/sota.csv
python -m simulation --architecture tise --reasoning probabilistic --steps 24 --seed 42 --output results/tise.csv
Then repeat with several seeds and compute mean, dispersion, worst case, and confidence intervals. Agentic runs should also report provider failures and fallback rates.

title: 4  Experiments, Evidence, and Interpretation
text:
4.5 Interpretation rules

Baseline success supports model existence, not optimality.
SOTA improvement supports the selected fixed contract, not all smart contracts.
TISE improvement in normal conditions supports adaptive value creation only for tested objectives and cases.
TISE resilience requires evidence across disruption types, severities, and durations.
A simulator demonstrates behavior of assumptions. It does not by itself validate those assumptions against the real world.

title: 4  Experiments, Evidence, and Interpretation
text:
4.6 Threats to validity checklist

Are stakeholder missions elicited from actual stakeholders?
Are VOMR units and update equations dimensionally valid?
Are safety constraints modeled as hard constraints?
Is the baseline calibrated to observations?
Are models compared using identical random seeds and scenarios?
Does the agent receive information unavailable in the real system?
Are provider prompts or model changes documented?
Are results robust to weights and objective functions?
Are distributional effects reported alongside aggregate wealth?

title: 5  Capstone Project
text:
5.1 Task
Design and simulate an MOEE model for one public-need ecosystem:

title: 5  Capstone Project
5  Capstone Project
text:
5.2 Required deliverables

A two-page case boundary and public-need statement.
Stakeholder registry with user, source, regulator/governance, and operator roles.
Mission and hard-constraint specification.
PSKVE exchange matrix.
VOMR accounts with units and update equations.
MIG definition and selected reasoning mode for every stakeholder.
Baseline, SOTA smart contract, and adaptive TISE specifications.
Normal and at least three disruptive scenarios.
Reusable Python implementation derived from simulation/.
Automated tests and reproducible run commands.
Results comparing value creation and resilience.
A limitations, safety, governance, and next-evidence section.

title: 5  Capstone Project
text:
5.3 Recommended milestones

Week 1: case boundary and stakeholders
Week 2: missions, PSKVE, and VOMR
Week 3: MIGs and architecture definitions
Week 4: baseline implementation and validation
Week 5: SOTA and TISE implementation
Week 6: experiments, analysis, and defense

title: 5  Capstone Project
text:
5.4 Rubric








Criterion
Points
Evidence




Public need and boundary
10
Clear population, place, horizon, exclusions


Stakeholders and missions
15
Complete roles, autonomy, constraints


PSKVE and VOMR
15
Traceable exchanges and valid accounts


MIG design
10
Meaning, units, reasoning, limits


Three architectures
10
Fair and correctly distinguished


Python design
15
Reusable classes, provider separation, readable code


Verification
10
Tests, seeds, reproducible commands


Experimental validity
10
Fair comparisons and disruption evidence


Safety and limitations
5
Appropriate governance and bounded claims

title: 5  Capstone Project
text:
5.5 Oral-defense questions

Which model assumption most strongly affects your result?
How do you know that a TISE gain is not simply caused by a stronger stakeholder reasoner?
Which stakeholder could lose while aggregate value rises?
Which PUDAL recommendation requires human authorization?
What observation would falsify your model?
What data would be required before real-world deployment?

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
text:
7 Purpose and outcome
This tutorial turns an engineering application case and its simulation into a focused, reproducible IEEE-style paper. The running example is the sustainable campus food ecosystem developed in this course, but the workflow also applies to transportation, health care, energy, education, and other engineered systems.
The key move is to treat the case and the simulation as evidence for a research claim, not as the paper’s claim by themselves. A publishable paper does more than describe an application or report program output. It states a gap, proposes a technical response, tests that response fairly, and limits its conclusions to what the evidence supports.
At the end of the tutorial, you should have:
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
section: 8.1 Convert the application into a research problem
text:
8.1 Convert the application into a research problem
Describe the application in five lines before writing prose:








Element
Question
Campus food example




Public need
What outcome matters, and to whom?
Affordable, safe, nutritious food for the campus community


System boundary
Which place, actors, flows, and time horizon are modeled?
One campus, its consumers, vendors, suppliers, operator, and governance actors


Technical difficulty
Why do existing methods struggle?
Stakeholders have different missions and disruptions change feasible exchanges


Proposed response
What mechanism is introduced?
Adaptive PUDAL coordination of stakeholder MIGs and PSKVE exchanges


Testable consequence
What should improve if it works?
Mission achievement and value retention improve without violating hard constraints



A research problem is stronger than a topic. “AI for campus food” is a topic. The following is testable:

Fixed coordination rules cannot respond to changing stakeholder missions and resource disruptions; therefore, this study tests whether adaptive multi-stakeholder coordination improves mission achievement and resilience under controlled, matched simulation conditions.
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
section: 8.2 Write the claim before the manuscript
text:
8.2 Write the claim before the manuscript
Use this pattern:

For [bounded application and conditions], the proposed [method] improves [primary outcomes] relative to [credible comparator], as measured by [declared metrics], while satisfying [constraints].

Example:

For a simulated campus food ecosystem under normal and supply-disruption scenarios, adaptive TISE coordination improves aggregate mission achievement and performance retention relative to baseline and fixed-contract coordination, while preserving stakeholder safety and resource constraints.

This statement is a hypothesis until results support it. If the simulation does not support it, revise the claim rather than hiding inconvenient results.
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
text:
8.3 Build a claim–evidence map
Every contribution must point to an artifact or result.








Proposed contribution
Required evidence
Likely paper location




Formal ecosystem representation
Defined stakeholders, missions, variables, exchanges, and constraints
Method/model


Adaptive coordination method
Algorithm, inputs, outputs, objective, and complexity or execution details
Method


Reusable simulation testbed
Architecture, parameters, scenarios, seeds, and code availability
Experimental setup


Performance or resilience improvement
Matched comparisons, uncertainty, effect sizes, and sensitivity tests
Results



Remove a claimed contribution if the paper provides no direct evidence for it.
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
text:
11.1 Title
Make the title specific enough to reveal the method, problem, and evidence type. Avoid claims such as “optimal,” “intelligent,” or “superior” unless they are precisely established.
Useful pattern:

[Method]: [Technical purpose] for [application], evaluated through [evidence]

Example:

Adaptive Multi-Stakeholder Coordination for Campus Food Ecosystems: A Digital-Twin Simulation Study
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
text:
11.2 Abstract
Write the abstract after the results are final. Use five moves in one paragraph:

Context: one sentence explaining why the problem matters.
Gap: one sentence naming the unresolved technical limitation.
Method: one or two sentences describing what was built.
Evaluation: one sentence stating comparators, scenarios, metrics, and scale.
Result and meaning: one or two sentences with the principal numerical result and a bounded conclusion.

Template:

[Context]. However, [specific limitation in existing approaches]. This paper proposes [method and essential mechanism]. We evaluate it in [application/model] against [comparators] across [scenarios, seeds, or conditions], using [primary metrics]. The proposed method achieves [main result with value and uncertainty] and [secondary result]. These findings indicate [bounded implication], although [important limitation, if needed].

Do not place citations, undefined abbreviations, background surveys, or unreported promises in the abstract.
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
text:
11.3 I. Introduction
A reliable six-paragraph introduction is:

Establish the real engineering need and its consequences.
Explain the technical difficulty, not merely the domain importance.
Summarize the closest solution families.
State precisely what remains unresolved.
Present the proposed response and evaluation logic.
List two to four verifiable contributions and preview the paper.

Contribution template:

The principal contributions are:

a formal [model] that represents [previously missing capability];
an adaptive [algorithm/mechanism] that [technical action] subject to [constraints]; and
a reproducible evaluation against [comparators] over [conditions], showing [supported result, added only after analysis].


Do not use “The paper is novel” as evidence of novelty. The comparison with prior work must make that case.
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
text:
11.4 II. Related Work
Organize related work by technical approach, not one paragraph per paper. For this study, useful groups might be mission-oriented engineering, multi-agent or multi-stakeholder coordination, digital twins, smart contracts, and resilience evaluation.
For each group, answer:

What problem does the approach solve?
What information and assumptions does it require?
What does it optimize or guarantee?
What remains unavailable for this paper’s problem?

End with a synthesis table whose columns encode the capabilities needed by the proposed claim. Cite original papers and authoritative standards where possible. A missing citation is not automatically a research gap; demonstrate that the missing capability matters technically.
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
section: 11.5 III. Application Case and Problem Formulation
text:
11.5 III. Application Case and Problem Formulation
Separate the real-world narrative from the mathematical abstraction.
First, define:

population, location, time step, and study horizon;
stakeholder roles and decision authority;
resources, outputs, exchanges, and external inputs;
normal operation and disruption boundaries; and
exclusions and assumptions.

Then formalize the model. At minimum identify:
[ ={s_1,,s_n}, _t=, _t=, _t=. ]
State the transition, objective, and constraints:
[ _{t+1}=f(_t,_t,_t), ]
[ {{0:T}} J={t=0}^{T}{i}w_i U_i(_t,_t), ]
subject to safety, capacity, conservation, fairness, and governance constraints. Define every symbol, unit, range, and data source. If weights express a policy preference rather than an observed fact, say so.
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
text:
11.7 V. Experimental Method
Write this section as a protocol that another researcher could repeat.

11.7.1 Research questions
Example questions are:

RQ1: Does adaptive coordination improve mission achievement in normal operation?
RQ2: Does it retain more performance and recover faster during disruptions?
RQ3: How sensitive are conclusions to objective weights, reasoning modes, and random seeds?
RQ4: Which stakeholders benefit or lose when aggregate performance changes?



11.7.2 Comparators and ablations
Use matched conditions for baseline, SOTA, and TISE. Add ablations that remove one proposed component at a time where feasible. Compare against the strongest relevant comparator, not an intentionally weak baseline.


11.7.3 Scenarios and parameters
Report normal operation plus named disruptions, including onset, duration, severity, and recovery. Provide a parameter table with symbol, meaning, unit, value or range, and justification. Separate measured values, literature-derived values, calibrated values, and assumptions.


11.7.4 Reproducibility
For this repository, a minimum matched run is:
python -m simulation --architecture baseline --reasoning probabilistic --steps 24 --seed 42 --output results/baseline.csv
python -m simulation --architecture sota --reasoning probabilistic --steps 24 --seed 42 --output results/sota.csv
python -m simulation --architecture tise --reasoning probabilistic --steps 24 --seed 42 --output results/tise.csv
Repeat stochastic conditions over multiple declared seeds. Record the software version, commit identifier, operating environment, dependencies, run command, configuration, raw outputs, and analysis script. If an external AI model is involved, also record provider, model identifier, prompt version, sampling settings, failures, fallbacks, and access date.


11.7.5 Metrics and analysis
Declare one primary metric before inspecting results. Secondary metrics can include stakeholder utility, total value, mission achievement, constraint violations, equity, retention, recovery time, cumulative loss, and computation time.
Report:

sample size and unit of analysis;
mean or median as appropriate;
dispersion or confidence intervals;
absolute and relative effect sizes;
statistical test and assumptions, if inferential tests are used; and
sensitivity and worst-case behavior.

Seeds are repeated simulation realizations, not automatically independent real-world observations. Avoid using a small simulation p-value as proof that the real system will behave identically.
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
text:
11.9 VII. Discussion and validity
The discussion explains what the results mean and why they occurred. A useful order is:

answer each research question;
explain the observed mechanism;
compare the result with prior work;
discuss practical significance and stakeholder tradeoffs;
report failure cases; and
bound generalization.

Address validity explicitly:

Construct validity: Do the metrics represent mission, value, resilience, and equity?
Internal validity: Are comparison differences caused by the treatment rather than unequal information or parameters?
External validity: Which conclusions may transfer beyond this campus and which depend on local assumptions?
Conclusion validity: Are there enough runs, appropriate summaries, and robust effects?

Simulation validates consequences of the encoded assumptions. Real-world claims require calibration, observational evidence, expert review, field trials, or other external validation.
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
text:
11.10 VIII. Conclusion
Use three short moves:

restate the problem and technical response;
report the main quantitative finding; and
state the bounded implication and next evidence needed.

Do not introduce a new method, citation, limitation, or result in the conclusion.
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
text:
16.1 Research argument

Is there one explicit research question and one primary claim?
Does each contribution have visible evidence?
Is the closest prior work compared fairly?
Are claims bounded to the modeled case and tested conditions?
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
text:
16.2 Experimental integrity

Are comparators run with matched information, parameters, scenarios, and seeds?
Are parameters sourced or labeled as assumptions?
Are verification, uncertainty, ablation, sensitivity, and failure cases reported?
Can every reported number be regenerated from stored data and code?
Are stakeholder-level effects reported alongside aggregate outcomes?
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
text:
16.3 Manuscript quality

Are all acronyms defined at first use?
Does every symbol have a meaning and unit?
Does every figure and table have a takeaway caption and a textual reference?
Are citations complete and generated from the bibliography rather than typed manually?
Does the abstract contain the actual principal numerical result?
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation
text:
16.4 Submission

Does the manuscript match the selected venue’s scope and current author instructions?
Are page limit, anonymity, ethics, conflict, funding, and data/code declarations satisfied?
Are fonts embedded and figures legible in the final two-column PDF?
Have all comments, placeholders, tracked changes, and identifying metadata been handled?
Has every author reviewed and approved the final manuscript?
crumbs: 6  Writing an IEEE Paper from an Application Case and Simulation

title: 7  Instructor Guide
text:
7.1 Teaching approach
Use one concept–artifact–code loop in every session:
Avoid beginning with the AI provider. Learners should first produce a coherent baseline using historical reasoning. Otherwise, model-generated decisions can conceal missing missions, invalid accounts, or undefined objectives.

title: 7  Instructor Guide
7  Instructor Guide
text:
7.2 Suggested class pattern

20 minutes: concept and worked example
25 minutes: team modeling exercise
25 minutes: Python lab
15 minutes: comparison and critique
5 minutes: exit ticket

title: 7  Instructor Guide
text:
7.3 Lab progression

lab01_baseline.py: validate execution and VOMR updates.
lab02_compare_architectures.py: isolate coordination effects.
lab03_agentic_tise.py: add a provider behind an interface.
lab04_custom_case.py: transfer MOEE to elderly home-care coordination.

title: 7  Instructor Guide
text:
7.4 Discussion prompts

Who is authorized to define the ecosystem objective?
When is a smart contract preferable to adaptive control?
Which intelligence channel is absent from the available data?
What should PUDAL recommend but never execute automatically?
How should a stakeholder reject or renegotiate a recommendation?

title: 7  Instructor Guide
text:
7.5 Safety notes
Health care, elderly services, education involving minors, and regulated transportation require stronger safeguards than the toy simulator provides. Treat the package as a research and teaching instrument. It lacks production identity, privacy, consent, security, clinical, legal, and operational-assurance controls.

title: 7  Instructor Guide
text:
7.6 Instructor setup
Before class:
python -m unittest discover -s simulation/tests -v
python -m simulation --architecture tise --reasoning hybrid --provider heuristic --steps 6
quarto render course
Use the heuristic provider for guaranteed offline delivery. Demonstrate an external provider only when credentials, network access, cost controls, and data policy are already approved.
